context: 2 Kings chapter 7 this morning, 2 Kings chapter number 7, 2 Kings chapter number 7

context: the account of these four leprous men that begins for us in verse number 3

context: a Lord on whose hand the king lean answered the man of God and said, behold, if the Lord would make windows in heaven, might this thing be?
point_summary: Even amid God's certain provision, doubters will always exist—as this officer's skeptical response to Elisha's prophecy illustrates—and such unbelief carries its own consequence.

context: in verse number 16, we see the fulfillment of the prophecy. The Bible says, and the people went out and spoiled the tents of the Syrians

context: So notice what it says in verse 16. So a measure of fine flour was sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel

context: the people trod upon him in the gate, and he died. As the man of God had said
point_summary: The doubter who mocked Elisha's prophecy lived to see its exact fulfillment in verse 17 but died without partaking of it, illustrating that God's word comes to pass regardless of unbelief.

context: And there were four leprous men at the entering in of the gate. And they said one to another, why sit we here until we die?
point_summary: Four leprous men facing certain death from famine and leprosy reason through their only viable options, illustrating the desperate human condition that makes the gospel's offer of life urgent.

context: and they rose up in the twilight to go into the camp of the Syrians

context: for the Lord had made the host of the Syrians to hear a noise of chariots and a noise of horses, even the noise of a great host
point_summary: God's sovereign intervention is highlighted as the explanation for the empty Syrian camp — the Lord caused the enemy army to hear phantom sounds of chariots and horses, driving them to flee.

context: therefore, they arose and fled in the twilight and left their tents and their horses and their asses, even the camp as it was and fled for their life
point_summary: God's supernatural intervention — making the Syrians hear phantom armies — caused them to flee in panic, abandoning their entire camp, fulfilling what no human strategy could have accomplished.

context: we do not well. We do not well. Why, for this day is a day of good tidings and we hold our peace
point_summary: Possessing good news while others remain in ignorance is itself a moral failure—these lepers concluded on their own, without any command, that silence was wrong.

context: if we tarry till the morning light, some mischief will come upon us. Now therefore come, that we may go and tell the king's household
point_summary: Possessing good news while others remain in ignorance creates a moral obligation to share it—these lepers concluded on their own, without any commanding authority, that silence was inexcusable.

context: back in chapter number six and verse number 25, notice what the Bible says in 2 Kings 6 and verse 25, and there was a great famine in Samaria. And behold, they besieged it until an ass's head was sold for four score pieces of silver and the fourth part of a cab of doves dung for five pieces of silver
point_summary: Extreme famine conditions in Samaria—an ass's head sold at an exorbitant price—establish the desperate need the lepers understood when they resolved to bring news of provision to the city.

context: In verse 26, as the king of Israel was passing by upon the wall
point_summary: The famine's desperation—a starving woman crying to a powerless king—establishes the dire condition of Samaria's inhabitants that the lepers understood and that compelled them to go and tell.

context: the king of Israel was passing by upon the wall. There cried a woman unto him saying, help my Lord, O king
point_summary: The famine in Samaria had reached a level of despair so extreme — mothers bartering their own children for food — that the people's desperate need for deliverance was beyond human comprehension or remedy.

context: this woman said unto me, give thy son that we may eat him today. And we will eat my son tomorrow
point_summary: The famine in Samaria had reached a depth of despair so extreme — mothers bargaining to cannibalize their own children — that no modern claim of hunger can compare to what the city's inhabitants were enduring.

context: So we boiled my son and did eat him. And I said unto her on the next day, give thy son that we may eat him. And she hath hid her son.
point_summary: The famine in Samaria had reached a depth of desperation—mothers boiling and eating their own children—that the lepers knew those starving people urgently needed the good news they had found.

context: We do not well. Oh, we're full. We've had opportunity to hide gold and silver and raiment. We have all of this spoil. We could go to more tents and more tents, but we do not well. Why? It's a day of good tidings and we hold our peace.
point_summary: Possessing good news while others perish in desperate need makes silence morally indefensible — "we do not well" — and demands immediate declaration.

context: verses 10 and 11, their declaration. The Bible says in verse number 10, I love these three words. So they came.
point_summary: Knowing the desperate need of others obligates action — the lepers' arrival ("so they came") illustrates that good intentions only count when they are actually carried out.

context: as we read verses 10 and 11 and we noticed their declaration
point_summary: The lepers' arrival at the city gate to report the abandoned Syrian camp illustrates that genuine decisions must be followed by action — good intentions require feet.

context: we came to the camp of the Syrians and behold, there was no man there, neither voice of man, but horses tied and asses tied and the tents as they were
point_summary: The lepers' arrival at the city gate to report their discovery illustrates that genuine decisions must be followed by action — faith carried out on its feet, not left at the point of resolution.

context: He called the porters and they told it to the king's house within, according to verse number 11
point_summary: Good news demands immediate action, as the lepers' decision to report the abandoned Syrian camp was not left as mere intention but carried out with feet, excitement, and declaration that reached the king's house.

context: I mentioned to you in verse number 2 that there was a doubter to the message that Elisha
point_summary: Doubt of God's word is inevitable, as illustrated by the doubter in verse 2 and echoed in the king's skepticism, warning that unbelief will always resist the message.

context: if the Lord do not help thee

context: The Bible says in verse 14, obviously the king granted permission for it says they took therefore two chariot horses and the king sent after the host of the Syrians saying, go and see.

context: they went after them unto Jordan and lo all the way was full of garments and vessels which the Syrians had cast away in their haste

context: the messengers returned and told the king and the people went out and spoiled the tents of the Syrians
point_summary: God's word through Elisha proved literally true when the people went out and the market prices of flour and barley were fulfilled exactly as prophesied.

context: a measure of fine flour was sold for a shekel and two measures of barley for a shekel according to the word of the Lord
point_summary: God's word through Elisha proved literally true when the people went out and spoiled the Syrian camp, buying flour and barley at exactly the prices he had prophesied.

context: what those leprous men said, we do not well. Why? Because it's a day of glad tidings and we hold our peace
point_summary: Silence about the gospel when others face eternal peril is condemned by the lepers' own words — "we do not well" — because possessing glad tidings obligates believers to go and tell.

context: The oil with Elisha. What do you have in the house? Nothing, just a little pot of oil
point_summary: Childlike trust in God is warranted because, unlike a parent who might drop a child, God has never failed and never will — making unbelief without excuse.

context: there are more for us than with them
point_summary: God's unseen protection—illustrated by missionary accounts of angelic guardians and near-miss accidents—is grounded in the Elisha principle that the forces with God's people outnumber those against them.

context: we're going to look at a portion of scripture in 2 Kings chapter number three
point_summary: 2 Kings 3 is introduced as a historical narrative illustrating God doing what only God can do, with verse 18 anchoring the sermon's theme of divine ability exceeding human expectation.

context: and this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord

context: The Bible says in verse one, now Jehoram, the son of Ahab, began to reign over Israel and Samaria in the 18th year of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, and reigned 12 years.

context: we read in verse three, it says, nevertheless, he cleaved under the sins of
point_summary: Even a king who partially reforms—removing Baal's image—can still cling to entrenched national sins, as Jehoram's adherence to Jeroboam's wickedness illustrates.

context: as God gives us the setting here in these first three verses of 2 Kings three
point_summary: Jehoram's reign is introduced as the historical setting for the passage, noting he was less wicked than Ahab and Jezebel yet still clung to the entrenched sins of Jeroboam.

context: The rebellion is recorded for us in verses four and five. The Bible says that Misha, king of Moab, was a sheep master and he rendered unto the king of Israel, that would be Ahab, and 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool.
point_summary: The massive tribute Mesha of Moab paid to Ahab—100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with wool—establishes the economic stakes that make his subsequent rebellion a crisis demanding Jehoram's military response.

context: it says 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool. But after Ahab dies and Jehoram becomes the king of Israel, it tells us in verse number five, but it came to pass when Ahab was dead that the king of Moab rebelled against the king of Israel.

context: he, the king of Israel, Jehoram, that he went out of Samaria the same time and numbered all Israel
point_summary: Jehoram's mustering of Israel at Samaria is read as a standard pre-war census, signaling that he was calculating military readiness before seeking Jehoshaphat's alliance against Moab's rebellion.

context: he went and sent to Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, and here's what he says. The king of Moab hath rebelled against me. Wilt thou go with me against Moab to battle?

context: I will go up. I am as thou art, my people as thy people, and my horses as thy horses
point_summary: Jehoram's mustering of Israel and appeal to Jehoshaphat illustrates how a compromised king builds a military coalition, drawing the otherwise commendable Jehoshaphat into a questionable alliance.

reference_text: I am as thou art, my people as thy people, and my horses as thy horses
context: He says, I am as thou art, my people as thy people, and my horses as thy horses
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's willingness to join Jehoram's coalition against Moab—declaring "I am as thou art"—is presented as one of the good king's notable poor decisions, echoing his earlier ill-fated alliance with Ahab.

context: in verse number eight, there's a question asked with regard to which way they would go up
point_summary: The route chosen through the wilderness of Edom, answering the strategic question of verse 8, sets the stage for a three-king coalition whose military planning will soon encounter a crisis requiring divine intervention.

reference_text: which way shall we go up
context: he said, which way shall we go up? And he answered, the way through the wilderness of Edom
point_summary: Verse 8's question — "which way shall we go up?" — marks the strategic planning stage of the coalition, as the route through the wilderness of Edom is chosen to bring three kings against Moab.

context: they fetched a compass of seven days journey
point_summary: The crisis of no water for the allied army after seven days' march illustrates how human strategy can be fully set in motion before a desperate need exposes the failure to seek God first.

context: the king of Israel said, alas, that the Lord hath called these three kings together to deliver them into the hand of Moab
point_summary: Jehoram's panicked conclusion that God had summoned the three kings only to destroy them illustrates how a faithless perspective reads crisis as divine abandonment rather than an occasion to seek God.

context: the word of the Lord is with him
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's confident declaration that "the word of the Lord is with him" reveals that Elisha's prophetic authority was already established and recognized, bringing immediate encouragement to the desperate kings.

context: and Elisha said unto the king of Israel, what have I to do with thee?
point_summary: Elisha's sharp rebuke of Jehoram — "What have I to do with thee?" — exposes that God's prophet had no obligation to a king who served idols and never sought the Lord.

context: He followed after the sins of Jeroboam according to verse number three
point_summary: Jehoram's evil, idol-serving character—established by his following Jeroboam's sins—is the reason Elisha sharply distances himself from him, directing him instead to his father's prophets.

reference_text: Get thee to the prophets of thy father and to the prophets of thy mother
context: Get thee to the prophets of thy father and to the prophets of thy mother
point_summary: Elisha's sharp rebuke of Jehoram — "What have I to do with thee?" — reflects that the idolatrous king had no standing before God's prophet, having pursued false gods rather than the Lord.

reference_text: the Lord hath called these three kings together to deliver them into the hand of Moab
context: the Lord hath called these three kings together to deliver them into the hand of Moab
point_summary: Elisha's rebuke of the idolatrous Jehoram establishes that God's prophet had no obligation to the wicked king, and only Jehoshaphat's faithful presence secured any divine audience at all.

context: as the Lord of hosts liveth before whom I stand, surely were it not that I regard the presence of Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, I would not look toward thee nor see thee
point_summary: Elisha's willingness to help Jehoram only because of Jehoshaphat's presence illustrates that a faithful believer's walk with God can bring blessing and divine attention to those around them who do not deserve it on their own.

context: that we may inquire of the Lord by him
point_summary: Seeking a prophet was explicitly motivated by the desire to inquire of the Lord, establishing that access to divine guidance—not merely human strategy—was the kings' stated purpose.

context: but now bring me a minstrel, and it came to pass when the minstrel played, the hand of the Lord came upon him
point_summary: When the minstrel played and the hand of the Lord came upon Elisha, God gave specific directional instruction — "make this valley full of ditches" — demonstrating that seeing God work begins with divine guidance through his prophet.

context: thus saith the Lord, make this valley full of ditches
point_summary: God's command to dig ditches distinguishes between what only He can do—supply water supernaturally—and what He assigns to human hands, requiring active preparation before the miracle comes.

context: For thus saith the Lord, ye shall not see wind, neither shall you see rain. Yet that valley shall be filled with water that you may drink both ye and your cattle and your beast
point_summary: God's command to dig ditches without any visible sign of rain illustrates that humans are responsible to do what they can do, while only God can produce what they cannot.

reference_text: verse number nine of seven days journey
context: they fetched a compass according to verse number nine of seven days journey and still dry, no water
point_summary: God's provision of water after seven days of fruitless searching is framed as "a light thing in the sight of the Lord," contrasting human desperation with divine ease.

context: as we mentioned earlier this evening in verse 18, and this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord
point_summary: What appears to humanity as an overwhelming, impossible need — water with no wind, no rain, no natural explanation — God himself dismisses as a light thing, underscoring the vast disproportion between human crisis and divine capability.

context: it's but a light thing in the sight of the Lord
point_summary: What appears to human eyes as an overwhelming, impossible need — water for an entire army — God himself describes as a light thing, underscoring that no crisis exceeds his ability to act.

context: it came to pass in the morning when the meat offering was offered that behold, there came water by the way of Edom and the country was filled with water
point_summary: God's fulfillment of Elisha's promise — water filling the country at the time of the morning offering — is presented as evidence that God always does what He says He will do and is able to deliver on every promise.

context: the country was filled with water
point_summary: God's fulfillment of Elisha's promise — water filling the country by morning — is presented as evidence that God always accomplishes exactly what He says He will do.

context: he gave victory over the Moabites
point_summary: When God's miraculous provision of water is seen by the enemy, the Moabites mobilize in response, shifting the narrative from Israel's crisis to the coming deliverance God had already promised.

context: and when all the Moabites heard that the kings were come up to fight against them, they gathered all that were able to put on armor and upward and stood in the border
point_summary: When the Moabites mobilized in response to the advancing coalition, God had already set in motion a provision that would simultaneously meet Israel's need and secure their deliverance.

context: and they rose up early in the morning
point_summary: God's foreknowledge encompassed even the sun shining on the water, so that the very provision meant to sustain Israel became the instrument of Moab's deception and defeat.

context: came to pass in the morning when the meat offering was offered, that behold, there came water by the way of Edom and the country was filled with water
point_summary: God's faithfulness is confirmed in the fulfillment of his word: the country was filled with water exactly as promised, demonstrating that he both directs and provides.

context: and the sun shone upon the water
point_summary: God's foreknowledge of the sun shining on the water turned a natural phenomenon into a divinely orchestrated deception that lured the Moabites into a fatal ambush.

context: this is blood, the kings are surely slain and they have smitten one another and therefore Moab to the spoil
point_summary: God used the Moabites' misreading of sunlit water as blood to draw them into a trap, turning their false conclusion into the very means of their defeat.

context: when they came to the camp of Israel, the Israelites rose up and smote the Moabites
point_summary: The Moabites' fatal misreading of the sun-reddened water as blood lured them into Israel's camp, where God turned their presumed advantage into their defeat.

context: that was the instruction that God had given to them
point_summary: God's precise instructions in verse 19 — destroying cities, stopping wells, felling trees — were fulfilled exactly as commanded, confirming that the victory unfolded according to his predetermined word.

context: when the king of Moab saw that the battle was too sore for him
point_summary: When the king of Moab found the battle too fierce to escape, his desperate attempt to break through with 700 swordsmen failed, illustrating that God's deliverance of the coalition was total and inescapable.

context: then he, the king of Moab, took his eldest son that should have reigned in his stead and offered him for a burnt offering upon the wall
point_summary: Desperate human strategy—here, a king sacrificing his own son—stands in tragic contrast to the God-given victory the coalition experienced when they finally sought the Lord rather than relying on their own plans.

context: You know what we find in 2 Kings chapter number three? There was no way that Jehoram, Jehoshaphat, the King of Edom, any of the hosts, there was no way that any of them could take credit for getting the water
point_summary: In 2 Kings 3, God supplied water in a way that left no human able to claim credit, illustrating that obedient preparation (digging ditches) precedes divine intervention that only God can accomplish.

context: What about Hezekiah, 2 Kings chapter number 20, look there, if you will
point_summary: Hezekiah's story is introduced as a case study in divine healing, with his terminal illness and the subsequent fifteen additional years framing both God's healing power and the sobering responsibility of finishing one's life well.

context: in those days was Hezekiah sick unto death. The prophet Isaiah, the son of Amos, came to him and said unto him, thus saith the Lord, set thine house in order, for thou shalt die and not live
point_summary: Hezekiah's terminal diagnosis and subsequent healing serve as a direct biblical illustration that God is Jehovah Rapha—the God who heals—responding to desperate, tearful prayer.

context: Verse two, then he turned his face to the wall and prayed unto the Lord.
point_summary: Faced with a death sentence, Hezekiah turned to the wall and prayed, and God responded by hearing his prayer, seeing his tears, and promising healing—demonstrating that God is able to heal.

context: He says in verse three, I beseech thee, O Lord, remember now how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart, and have done that which is good in thy sight.
point_summary: Hezekiah's appeal to his faithful walk before God—in truth and with a perfect heart—is presented as the ground of his prayer for healing, showing that he turned to the only One able to heal him.

context: behold, was he saying, I will heal thee. That's what your Bible says. God says I will heal thee. On the third day thou shalt go up unto the house of the Lord, and I will add unto thy days fifteen years.
point_summary: God's direct response to Hezekiah's tearful prayer—"I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears; I will heal thee"—grounds the sermon's claim that God is able and that prayer is the proper response to sickness.

context: Ahaziah, 2 Kings chapter number 1
point_summary: Ahaziah's response to sickness serves as a negative example: rather than turning to Jehovah Rapha, he sent messengers to inquire of a foreign god instead.

context: it's chapter 2 where he'll be caught up in that whirlwind with the chariot of fire
point_summary: Ahaziah's failure to seek Jehovah Rapha—turning to a pagan god rather than the Lord when sick—is presented as a negative contrast to Hezekiah's prayer and God's healing response.

context: chapter number 1 that we're given this information with regard to Ahaziah. Notice in verse number 1 of 2 Kings chapter 1, the Bible says, then Moab rebelled against Israel
point_summary: Ahaziah's turn to Baal-Zebub in his sickness rather than to the God of Israel is presented as the defining negative example of rejecting Jehovah Rapha as the true source of healing.

context: and after the death of Ahab, in verse 2, and Ahaziah fell down through a lattice in his upper chamber that was in Samaria, and he was sick
point_summary: Ahaziah's turn to Baal-Zebub in his sickness rather than to Israel's God is presented as the paradigmatic negative response to illness — seeking healing from a false god instead of from Jehovah Rapha.

context: Verse 3, But the angel of the Lord said to Elijah the Tishbite, Arise, go up to meet the messengers of the king of Samaria
point_summary: Ahaziah's consultation of Baal-Zebub rather than Israel's God illustrates the negative response of seeking healing from a false source instead of Jehovah Rapha, the true healer.

context: Is it not because there is not a god in Israel that you go to inquire of Baal Zebub, the god of Ekron
point_summary: God's rebuke through Elijah to Ahaziah's messengers establishes that seeking healing from false gods rather than Jehovah—who had already revealed himself as the God who heals—is a failure to trust the only true God.

context: Think of Hezekiah in 2 Kings, in chapter number 19. In 2 Kings chapter 19, Hezekiah is in a position that's a difficult position
point_summary: Hezekiah's difficult position in 2 Kings 19 is introduced as an example of how knowing God produces the courage and confidence to pray bold, trusting prayers in impossible circumstances.

context: Notice with me in 2 Kings here in chapter 19 and verse number 14
point_summary: Hezekiah's act of spreading the threatening letter before the Lord illustrates that knowing who God is produces the courage and confidence to bring impossible situations directly to Him.

context: And Hezekiah received the letter of the hand of the messengers and read it. And Hezekiah went up into the house of the Lord and spread it before the Lord.
point_summary: Hezekiah's act of spreading Sennacherib's threatening letter before the Lord illustrates that the proper response to overwhelming opposition is to bring it directly into God's presence in prayer.

context: And he says in verse 15, Hezekiah prayed before the Lord and said, O Lord God of Israel, which dwellest between the cherubims, thou art the God, even thou alone.
point_summary: Facing Assyrian threats that had toppled nations and their gods, Hezekiah's prayer distinguishes Israel's God as the sole Creator over all kingdoms, the living God whom no earthly power can reproach.

context: And have cast their gods, little g, idols, false gods, into the fire. For they were no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone. Therefore they have destroyed them.
point_summary: Hezekiah's concession that Assyria's gods were destroyed because they were merely wood and stone—no gods at all—sharpens his appeal: only the living God can save where idols cannot.

context: Now, therefore, O Lord. Lord our God, I beseech thee, save thou us out of his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that thou art the Lord God, even thou only.
point_summary: Hezekiah's repeated acknowledgment of who God is within his prayer served not as theological formality but as the source of his own courage, comfort, and confidence amid crisis.

context: You read the book of First Kings, Second Kings
point_summary: The repeated pattern in 1–2 Kings of children abandoning the faith of their parents illustrates the urgent need for each generation to own a living, personal relationship with God rather than inheriting a secondhand one.
